summary: Jeff Atwood explores the concept of egoless programming, drawing from Jerry Weinberg's classic 'The Psychology of Computer Programming.' The core idea is that developers should separate their self-worth from their code, enabling them to accept criticism and learn from mistakes during peer reviews. Atwood acknowledges a paradox: while many workers are apathetic about their jobs, passionate programmers often have the opposite problem—they care too much, treating their code like intimate love letters. This excessive attachment makes criticism feel personal and hinders collaboration. The solution is embracing reviews as learning opportunities rather than attacks on one's identity.
key_points: Egoless programming means tying your ego to your effort and learning, not to the perfection of your output
Peer reviews work best when everyone expects to both produce and find errors
Passionate programmers often struggle with ego because they care deeply about their craft
Early programming culture treated code as private property, making it unintelligible to outsiders
Disconnecting self-worth from job function makes it easier to embrace criticism
money_quotes: My ego is not tied to my 'perfect' or 'imperfect' work product. My ego is only tied to my attempts to do the best job I know how, and to learn from my mistakes, not the initial result of my work.
You are not your job. You are not how much money you have in the bank.
It's a lot easier to embrace criticism of your work when you don't let your work define who you are.
Those of us who love programming enough to become highly skilled at it tend to have the opposite problem – we care too much.
A program was regarded as the private property of the programmer. One would no more think of reading a colleague's program unbidden than of picking up a love letter and reading it.
This is essentially what a program was, a love letter from the programmer to the hardware, full of the intimate details known only to partners in an affair.
key_insight: The best programmers must learn to separate their identity from their code, channeling their passion into continuous improvement rather than defensive attachment to their work.
